title: Montreal-style bagels
subtitle: Honey-water bath, wood-oven crumb
excerpt: Smaller and sweeter than their New York cousins, Montreal bagels are poached in honey-sweetened water and baked in a very hot oven until the sesame crust is properly set. The crumb is dense and chewy, the ring tight.
sourceNotes: Montreal bagel tradition documented in Marcy Goldman, A Treasury of Jewish Holiday Baking (1998), and in the oral record of Fairmount and St-Viateur bakeries. The honey-water poach and sesame finish are the two defining differences from the New York style. The method here follows the traditional Montreal hand-roll approach with a standard domestic oven at high heat.

content.text: Montreal bagels are smaller, denser, and sweeter than their New York counterpart. Where the New York bagel is broad and pillow-soft, the Montreal version is a tight ring with a pronounced chew, a sesame crust, and a crumb that holds up to a smear of cream cheese without collapsing. The defining step is the
honey poach
, hot water laced with honey that firms the surface before the bagel goes into the oven.
This recipe uses 500 g of plain flour at 55% hydration, a firm dough that
hand-rolling

works well. The sesame coating goes on before baking; white sesame for the classic finish, but a half-and-half mix of white and black reads beautifully. Plan about two hours from mixing to the first bagel out of the oven.
Ingredients

attrs.prepNote: in the dough; plus 3 tbsp for the poaching water
white, or a mix of white and black

content.text: Method
Mix and knead

Dissolve the yeast in the lukewarm water, then stir in the honey, beaten egg, and oil. Add the flour and salt and bring the dough together. It will feel stiff compared to a sandwich loaf dough. Turn it out and knead for 10 minutes by hand until smooth and slightly tacky. At 55% hydration this is a firm dough; it should hold its shape without spreading.
Bulk ferment, 1 hour

Place the dough in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with a damp cloth, and leave at room temperature for around 1 hour until risen by half. It won't double the way a wetter sandwich dough does; look for a noticeable puff and a surface that springs back slowly when pressed.
Shape

Divide the dough into 12 equal pieces, about 65 g each. Roll each piece into a rope about 25 cm long, slightly thicker in the middle. Wrap the rope around your palm and press the ends firmly together, a 2 to 3 cm overlap works well. The
hand-roll method
gives a tighter ring than the thumb-poke method. Place the shaped rings on a lightly oiled baking tray.
Proof, 20 minutes

Cover loosely and rest at room temperature for 20 minutes. They'll puff slightly but not dramatically. Preheat your oven to 240°C conventional (or 220°C fan) during this rest. Domestic ovens drift by 20°C or more; an oven thermometer takes the guessing out of a bake where the crust sets in the first few minutes.
Poach

Bring a wide saucepan of water to a rolling boil. Add 3 tablespoons of honey and stir to dissolve. Working in batches of three or four, lower the bagels into the water and poach for 1 minute per side. They'll bob to the surface and look slightly puffed. Lift out with a slotted spoon, let any excess water drip away, then press one side firmly into the sesame seeds before placing on a lined baking tray, seed side up.
Bake

Bake at 240°C for 18 to 22 minutes, until the crust is a deep amber-gold and the sesame has set without scorching. The bagels should feel firm and sound hollow when tapped on the base. Cool on a wire rack for at least 10 minutes before slicing; cutting a hot bagel compresses the crumb.
Troubleshooting
Bagels unravel in the water: the join wasn't pressed firmly enough. Press the ends together harder and roll the joint on the work surface to seal it before proofing.
Crust pale after baking: oven too cool. Add 5 minutes and check again, or confirm with an oven thermometer.
Crumb too dense: underproofed, or dough too cool at mixing. Use lukewarm (not cold) water and give the bulk ferment a full hour in a warm kitchen.
